content.text: hybrid of standard Claude API token costs plus a runtime fee for active session time
. This means an agent that runs continuously 24/7 costs roughly
in instance fees alone ($0.08/hour × 24 hours × 30 days ≈ $57.60), plus whatever you pay for Claude tokens consumed during that time.

attrs.alt: Pricing and cost model (~$58/month per 24/7 agent) diagram

content.text:
Claude Managed Agents charges two types of costs:
-

: Standard Claude API rates for prompt and completion tokens (same as the regular API).
-
while the agent is executing in the sandbox.

The "~$58/month per 24/7 agent" figure comes purely from the instance cost:
- $0.08/hour × 24 hours/day × 30 days ≈
for a continuously running agent, regardless of token usage.
If the agent also consumes tokens (e.g., 100k input tokens + 50k output tokens per month at typical Claude rates), you add those token costs on top of the $58 base.
The runtime fee reflects that Managed Agents is a

, not just a chat API. You're paying for:
-

: Secure execution environments with pre-installed packages and network access.
-

: The platform handles tool execution, context management, and error recovery.
-
: Conversation history, filesystem state, and outputs are stored server-side.

: You pay mostly for tokens; runtime is negligible.
-

: You pay significantly for active runtime, which can be the dominant cost.
-
: Runtime fees become around $58/month per agent, plus token costs.
= "body costs" (how long the agent body is actively running in the sandbox).
The $58/month figure is the cost of keeping the agent's "body" active 24/7, regardless of how much it thinks.

This pricing is best for workloads that need:
-

: Tasks that run for minutes or hours with multiple tool calls.
-

: Secure sandboxes with pre-installed packages and network access.
-

: Persistent filesystems and conversation history across interactions.
-
: No need to build your own agent loop, sandbox, or tool execution layer.

Managed Agents is more expensive per hour than just paying for tokens, because you're also paying for the managed infrastructure. But you save the 3–6 months of engineering time to build your own sandbox, agent loop, and state management.
Token cost: 50k input + 20k output tokens ≈ $1–$3 (depending on model).
: ~$5–$15 (token cost still dominates, but runtime is noticeable).
Token cost: 1M input + 500k output tokens ≈ $30–$90 (depending on model).
This is where the "$58/month per 24/7 agent" figure comes from: the runtime cost for a continuously running agent.
If the agent is "waiting" but not actively executing tools, you can
to stop charging runtime. When you resume, the agent continues from the same state.
2. Use cheaper models for simple tasks
to reduce token costs. Runtime costs are the same regardless of model.
Each tool call usually triggers a model request. Reducing unnecessary tool calls reduces token usage.
Instead of spreading work across many small sessions, batch tasks into fewer, longer sessions to reduce overhead.
If you need many 24/7 agents, self-hosted sandboxes might be cheaper at scale, but you pay for orchestration engineering time.
standard token rates + $0.08 per active session-hour
are the same as the regular Claude API;
are the additional Managed Agents cost.
where you don't want to build your own infrastructure.
Understanding the internals of pricing and cost model (~$58/month per 24/7 agent) helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with pricing and cost model (~$58/month per 24/7 agent) has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying pricing and cost model (~$58/month per 24/7 agent) in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Pricing and cost model (~$58/month per 24/7 agent) is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
